nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; coral-rich lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of 0–45 m

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding on filamentous algae, coral polyps, and benthic invertebrates. In aquaria, has been reported to accept finely chopped seafood, mysid shrimp, baby brine shrimp, frozen marine preparations, and algae/spirulina-based foods. Generally advised to feed small amounts at least 3 times daily, particularly during initial acclimation.

summary: An Indo-Pacific butterflyfish with a yellow-tan body marked by vertical rows of dark spots and seven dorsal bars. Generally considered not reef-safe due to a tendency to nip at corals and invertebrates; best suited to a spacious fish-only system with peaceful tankmates.
careNotes:
The Spotband Butterflyfish (Chaetodon punctatofasciatus) is generally considered an intermediate-level saltwater fish. It is commonly confused with the closely related Sunset Butterflyfish (Chaetodon pelewensis), which carries some of the same trade names — the two are sister species distinguishable mainly by spot orientation (vertical rows of spots in C. punctatofasciatus, diagonal rows in C. pelewensis) and overlap in parts of their ranges. It tends to be shy and docile and has been reported to do well singly or as a bonded pair if both individuals are introduced simultaneously. Like most Chaetodon species, it has been documented to form monogamous pair bonds in the wild — established pairs are generally advised to be kept together rather than separated. Numerous hiding places among live rock are important for reducing stress, and ample open swimming space is generally advised since this species has been described as an active swimmer that revels in cruising the reef.

This species is commonly considered not reef-safe. It has been documented preying on tubeworms, ornamental clams (including Tridacna), and both soft and LPS coral polyps. It should not be housed with corals, anemones, or sessile invertebrates.

Diet is broad — both meaty foods and vegetable matter (marine algae, spirulina) are generally advised, with feedings at least three times daily, especially during acclimation when refusing food is a common early problem.

Water parameter cross-source summary: Temperature ranged widely across sources (LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C / 72–78°F, FishBase 26.0–28.9°C, ReefApp ~26°C); the averaged range of 24–27°C is used here. Salinity: LiveAquaria SG 1.020–1.025, ReefApp ~1.026, web summary 1.022–1.026; averaged to 1.022–1.026. Tank minimum volume: LiveAquaria 70 gal (~265 L), ReefApp 300 L, with some hobby sources recommending considerably larger systems for active-swimming behavior; 300 L is used here as a sensible rounded value reflecting the active-swimming guidance. Adult size: LiveAquaria cites ~12.7 cm (5 in), FishBase records max TL 12 cm, ReefApp 12 cm — the 10–13 cm range is used here. No reliable captive lifespan data was found across the sources consulted.
breedingNotes: Generally regarded as not bred in captivity. In the wild has been reported to form monogamous pairs, with pelagic spawning typical of butterflyfishes; rearing of pelagic larvae has not been documented in the aquarium hobby.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm max TL), preferred temperature range (26.0–28.9°C), native range (Christmas Island to Line Islands, Ryukyu Islands to Great Barrier Reef), taxonomy (Acanthuriformes / Chaetodontidae), monogamous pairing, and depth range (0–45 m).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank volume (300 L), adult size (12 cm), salinity (~1.026), reef-safe status (not reef-safe — eats tubeworms, nibbles Tridacna clams, targets soft and LPS corals), and temperament (shy, docile). Used in averaging tank size and salinity disagreements.
